title: Tiny House Loft with Bed, Storage and Shelving
summary: A whole built-in loft system for a tiny house — a queen bed with under-bed drawers, a storage wall, a shelf, and a loft floor above, all tied into the structure.
description:
This is the most ambitious build in the catalog: not one piece of furniture but a complete integrated loft system for a tiny house — a queen platform bed with deep drawers underneath, a storage wall with a hidden compartment and tilt-out shoe cubbies, a loft floor overhead, and a pipe ladder and railing to reach it. It's carpentry as much as woodworking, and it's fastened into the house's actual framing, so treat it accordingly.

The backbone is a 4x4-post wall system lag-screwed to the studs — everything else hangs off that, so it has to be plumb, square, and genuinely anchored into framing, not just skin. The bed uses 2x8 mattress framing specifically to create a tall enough cavity for real drawers; if you skip the drawers, lighter 2x4 or 2x6 framing is fine. The loft floor uses 2x6 tongue-and-groove because the span over the doorway is close to 36" — don't cheap out to 1x material there.

Two honesty notes carried straight from the builder. First, every drawer and shoe cubby is built to fit its real opening and slides, not to a drawing — the listed sizes assume a perfect world. Second, the black-pipe ladder and railing are quick and good-looking but are explicitly not code-approved as guardrails; if people (especially kids) will use this loft, build the railing to a real guardrail spec and height. This is a permanent, load-bearing, fall-risk structure — build it to that standard.

tools.note: Handy for driving the 5" lag screws and long structural screws.
The whole system references the wall — it must go up plumb and level.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Wall framing, bed supports, wall cleats, loft floor framing.

materials.name: Black pipe (ladder and railing)
materials.note: Build the railing to a real guardrail spec — pipe as shown is not code-approved.

materials.name: 5" lag screws
materials.note: Anchoring the 4x4 posts to wall studs.

materials.name: Structural / pocket screws and nails
materials.note: 2-1/2" pocket screws, 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws, 1-1/4" nails.

cutList.note: One at 52", one at 71" — cut to your ceiling and loft heights.
76", 80", and length of room (e.g. 94-1/2") — measure your space.
Cut to fit your openings and slides — the given sizes assume perfect stock.
Cut to fit; 1/8" gap all around.

steps.title: Build and anchor the wall system
steps.body: Build the main wall system around the 4x4 posts — the best way to attach into the posts is with a Kreg jig, hiding pocket holes on the underside. This wall is the structural backbone; it must go up plumb and be lag-screwed into the house's wall studs with the 5" lags. Everything else references this wall, so get it right first.

steps.title: Build the bed support frame
steps.body: Build the 2x4 bed support frame with 2-3/4" self-tapping screws. Make this area very square — a racked bed frame throws off the drawers below it. Then attach the two frame pieces together.

steps.title: Level and attach the wall cleats
steps.body: Use a level to attach the wall cleats to the wall studs with 2-3/4" screws. These cleats carry the far edge of the bed and loft — level and stud-anchored, not into drywall.

steps.title: Frame the mattress platform with 2x8s
steps.body: Use 2x8s to support the mattress — they create a tall cavity for the under-bed drawers. (Plain 2x4 or 2x6 framing is fine if you skip the drawers.) If doing drawers, plan the framing around the drawer openings now.

steps.title: Panel the back of the wall
steps.body: Cover the back of the storage wall with your chosen material — leftover 1x6 tongue-and-groove, beadboard, paneling, or plywood all work. This finishes the compartment that sits behind the wall.

steps.title: Lay the loft floor
steps.body: Lay 2x6 tongue-and-groove for the loft floor above. Because the span over the doorway is close to 36", don't drop to 1x material here — the 2x6 T&G carries the span safely.

steps.title: Build the drawers to fit
steps.body: Build the under-bed drawers to fit their real openings and the 24" slides — the listed sizes assume a perfect build. For the tilt-out shoe cubbies, box out an area with 1x3s and back it with scrap 1/4" plywood.

steps.title: Install slides and hang drawers
steps.body: Install the drawer slides and hang the drawers, checking each for smooth, level travel before moving on.

steps.title: Add drawer faces and doors
steps.body: Attach the drawer faces and doors, keeping an even 1/8" gap on all sides when installed correctly. Hang doors on the hinges; add clasps or hasps if the hinges aren't self-closing.

steps.title: Add the bed slats
steps.body: For the bed slats, use a full-length board (front edge painted) for the first, visible slat, then use up any 3/4" material for the rest, splicing joints over the 2x8 framing so every slat is supported.

steps.title: Add the hidden storage door
steps.body: Hinge the 3/4" plywood hidden-storage door over the front opening. Cut a circle in the middle to act as a finger pull, so there's no hardware breaking the clean face.

steps.title: Build the ladder and railing — to spec
steps.body: Black iron pipe makes an easy, good-looking ladder and railing. But as shown it is NOT code-approved as a guardrail. If anyone — especially children — will use this loft, build the railing to a real guardrail height and baluster spacing. A fall from a loft is a serious injury; this is not the place to cut corners.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every reachable surface — this is a piece people climb into and touch daily — and finish to match your tiny-house interior. Confirm every structural connection is tight before you load it.

images.alt: A built-in tiny-house loft with a queen bed over storage drawers and a shelf wall
